questions.scenario:
The ECS service has this 30-day review:

```text
task size: 1024 CPU, 2048 MB memory
desired count: 6
normal CPU: 12 to 24 percent
normal memory: 18 to 33 percent
p95 latency: stable
5xx: stable
recent deploys: no task restart impact at count 4
```

What is a reasonable next step?

options.text: Test a smaller desired count or task size with monitoring and a rollback plan.
Set desired count to 1 immediately because low average CPU proves no resilience need.
Resize RDS first because ECS evidence cannot support ECS changes.
Disable alarms before testing so the change does not page anyone.

questions.explanation: Sustained low utilization with stable health can justify a careful right-sizing test. The change still needs a rollback path and post-change monitoring. Desired count also protects availability, so avoid jumping straight to one task.

questions.scenario:
RDS is the largest monthly line, but the review says:

```text
CPU mostly low
connections steady during normal traffic
write latency normal except export window
storage growing because monthly export metadata was retained
release week starts tomorrow
```

What is the best sequencing?

options.text: Do not resize during release week; first fix workload shape and review the instance after representative metrics.
Downsize immediately because RDS is the largest line in the snapshot.
Ignore storage growth because database cost cannot be affected by retained data.
Delete backups first because backups never affect recovery promises.

questions.explanation: RDS changes can be slower and riskier than ECS changes because the database holds shared state. The evidence suggests export behavior may be distorting the workload. Fix the shape first, then review sizing after a stable period.

questions.scenario:
S3 prefix usage shows:

```text
bucket: devpolaris-orders-prod-artifacts
receipts/: accessed by support for customer requests
exports/monthly/: needed for reporting window
exports/tmp/: rarely read after jobs finish, growing every week
```

Which right-sizing move is safest to investigate?

options.text: Add or adjust lifecycle for `exports/tmp/` after confirming the retry and audit window.
Apply one immediate expiration rule to the whole bucket because all prefixes have the same job.
Delete receipt objects first because they are older than temporary exports.
Move every object to logs so S3 storage no longer grows.

questions.explanation: Different prefixes have different jobs. Temporary export chunks may be safe to expire sooner, but receipts and monthly exports may have customer or reporting value. Lifecycle should follow retention intent, not one bucket-level rule.

questions.scenario: Which right-sizing habits are healthy for the orders service?

options.text: Compare utilization with latency, errors, target health, and deployment behavior.
Change one layer carefully and watch user-impact metrics after the change.
Keep recovery and observability requirements in the review before reducing retention or backups.
Reduce every layer at once so the team cannot tell which change caused a regression.

questions.explanation: Right-sizing works best when each change is evidence-based, scoped, and observable. User impact and recovery promises are part of the decision. Changing everything at once makes troubleshooting much harder.

questions.scenario:
Worker cost rises with SQS backlog. The worker review says:

```text
visible messages: rising
oldest message age: rising
worker errors: repeated ExportPayloadTooLarge
DLQ: empty
worker concurrency: increased after autoscaling reacted to backlog
```

What is the safest first control?

options.text: Reduce worker concurrency enough to stop the retry storm, fix payload splitting, then replay carefully.
Increase worker concurrency because more workers always make backlog healthier.
Delete the queue because an empty DLQ proves messages are safe to drop.
Resize the API tasks because all worker retries are caused by public HTTP traffic.

questions.explanation: A backlog caused by failing work is different from a backlog caused by useful work. More concurrency can make repeated failures more expensive and can pressure downstream services. First slow the failure loop, fix the code path, and replay with control.
